speaker: interviewer
text: Bernise Krantz. It's December the 1st, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Leon Greenman in London, England. And this is tape number four. You were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, each barrack had 900, 1,000, 1,200 1,100 men. And they had to be shaved every Saturday. So the kapos called out the ex-barbers, who were barbers and like myself, before we were taken prisoner. And then we were given razors, and we had to-- and, and, and clippers, and we had to clean the men up. That means shaving and cut the hair. Monday morning, when you march out to work, you have to have very short hair and clean face. Like a lot of zombies, we used to walk out, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was given then a, a razor. Well, I just want to tell you what comes in to mind. One of the Polish barbers, he said, go on, you're not a barber. Because there were thinking, so many barbers, then there's not enough soup, because they used to give you a little soup if there was any left. So every barber that came, it meant whatever it was was less. He said, go on. You're not a barber. I said, I am, man. What you talking about? I've got my own saloon, I used to have. So he said, all right, pick up that scissors. So I picked up the scissors. Yes, he says, you're a barber, because there's a certain way, the way you pick up a scissors. Thank the Lord for that. So I shaved there from Saturday morning, early, right throughout the day till in the evening. And if they were still left, I had to do Sunday morning, a few hours shaving. Now, there were men that used to lather, then you shift up and you shave it, quick, shaved. If you had a sharp razor, it went quick. And the others were clipping the hair. That went all day long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for that, if there was any soup, you got a little soup. And the soup was bad quality. It was water. But something to do-- still the hunger pain. That was life in, in Birkenau. If I think back, there was a long, long time before we could drink or, or even wash ourselves. Water-- you weren't allowed to drink the water. Water was bad in Birkenau. If you happen to drink the water, you became dysentery. And if you had dysentery, it meant your body was going wrong. You went into a barrack for dysentery. And if your body was not strong enough to get better, well, you ended up in the gas chamber. Of course, I didn't know all that. But everyone told don't drink the water, so you don't drink the water. Some did. There was a barrel of chlor-- chloride water to clean the other barrel in which we had to do our business. And there were two men, Scheissmeisters, who had to clean it every morning, or every other day, or every day. I don't s-- I can still see one or two men just dipping their hands in the chloride water to wet their lips. But I pulled pieces of ice from the window and sucked that, or pieces of grass between the snow on the ground. I used to pull that out and chew it until several weeks after that we could wash ourselves. We had to walk across the ground to another barrack. And in the barrack, you had a long kind of thing where horses used to drink from. That was the greatest water. We washed for the first time water. And I just taste that a little on my lips. I remember that. Anyhow, this wasn't a big selection in Birkenau. And we arrived in Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz, I was dealt into a kommando of 1,000 men, the Bauhof kommando, 1,000 men. At that time, I was alone. The Dutchmen went to other kommandos. And I couldn't talk Polish, very little German, and I felt a bit lonely. But I had to make, make, make the best of it. And we had a young kapo of that kommando, six foot-plus man. Young fella, maybe 20. Big fella. He was our kapo. I think he was Polish or Russian. And we had to, of course, be careful what you did or what not. And I remember one day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had forgotten to give up my razor on a Sunday afternoon, or perhaps it was on a Saturday night. After, after shaving, you've got to give up to the chief barber. Thank you. I put it in my pocket. Of course, it was too late. I fell asleep. And the next morning, out to work. And as it happened, another kapo used to get a hold of some of our prisoners, got them into the little wooden hut, and you had to empty your pockets. And they thought they could find something-- maybe tobacco or cigarettes or whatever. I had to empty my pockets, an out comes this razor. And a razor was a weapon. You can't allow that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, a razor? What, what's this? Where's your kapo? I said, well, there, in there. So he went to the kapo. He says, your man, he got a razor here. And those two didn't like one another. And did I cop out from my kapo. He nearly killed me, really. He knocked me to the ground. I was bleeding. He kicked me about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he-- the whole week went by, and he slept in the same barrack where we slept, where I slept. And the following week, he says to me, tomorrow, Monday, we're marching out to work. You got to shave me. I said, I've got no razor. He said, you keep your razor. Don't give it up. You shave me. I said, but you, you, you hit me before. Shut up. You take your razor with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to take the razor with me. And while he had nearly killed me, I had to now do voluntary to him. I shaved him in his wooden hut. Those things took place. He wasn't afraid you'd slit his throat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You couldn't do nothing. And of course, look, if they punish you-- if-- you could never hit back. You could never do nothing back. That, that still hurts me. You couldn't even look at the man when he was hitting you. Just don't look. Lay down. If you've got to get up, get up. You might get it again. But show yourself that you're really at his mercy. You must do that because otherwise, the other thousands, they see that-- oh, Leon is hitting back. We're going to hit back as well. And that was not allowed, of course. So you w-- they could do with you what you liked-- what you'd like. Apart from the SS, if they let the guns go in to your back. Hmm. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All right. I shaved this man then, and time went on. And I was sleeping in my barrack. That time underneath the bunk, underneath in the middle was the, the bunk where a Polish prisoner laid, and on the top, so it was three tiers again. And the man in the, middle he had a bandage around his head. He had been ill, and sometimes, after coming out of the sick barracks, they would allow you to stay in your own barrack for one or two days to recuperate so you don't work out-- walk out to work. And that morning, I said to this fellow then, in my broken German, see you tonight. And I went off to work with my kommando, and I was digging in the ground. The work was schlepping pieces of a barracks or building material. And that morning, we all stopped and looked in the distance. And in the distance, saw a long queue of men coming along, a lot of them. And as they came by, I noticed this man who I said good morning to. And he was carrying his wooden-- his cardboard box with something in it. The non-Jew could receive from family in Poland food, bread, wurst, fat, whatever they brought, garlic. [INAUDIBLE] and he passed by, and I waved to him, and he waved to me. I never saw him again. All that lot were gassed that same day, all that lot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was digging in the ground near a tree. And I dug up-- what do they call it, horseradish? This was a root, white. It was dirty. But I somehow cleaned a piece of it, and I ate it, horseradish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. That goes by now. I've been in Auschwitz camp six months altogether, six months in Auschwitz. But the first four months went by when I was called out of my barracks with one of my mates, and I was introduced to a Dutchman who had just came from Holland. And this man was Mr. Jacobs. And he introduced himself. He says, I'm Mr. Jacobs. I've been deported now. He said, I worked in Westerbork on the administration department.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how are things in Holland? He said, just as rotten as they were when you left. He said, but the name Greenman, I can never forget. I said, what's Greenman got to do with? You just met me. He says, I'll tell you. The morning that your train left, 15 minutes, 20 minutes, perhaps, after the train left, your name was paged. Greenman, come to the office. We found documentation acknowledging your British nationality. I said, how come? Now, this Kurt Schlesinger didn't open his mail before the train left. He could have saved a half a dozen families in my-- the same ways I was. He opened his mail after the train left, so he couldn't get us out. Now, he did it willingly or unwillingly, but he didn't do it right. And I hold ever-- held him responsible for the murder of my wife and child. That was Kurt Schlesinger.

speaker: interviewee
text: I searched for the man for years. If I only would have started earlier, I would have found him in Holland. But I never thought he was in Holland. I didn't know that-- if he was alive yet. But he escaped from Holland to America. That's another part of my talk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So you've been in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now two months added to Auschwitz, and again a selection. A selection means they're going to weed out the fit from the weak. And I'm with 200 men who seem to be all right. I used to be in a mo-- boxing club before the war, so I probably had the strong posture. And I was with 200 men selected to go to Monowitz. Monowitz is another three miles away from Auschwitz, a big camp, 10,000 men. The same life-- little food, beatings, hard work, unloading train loads with cement. One afternoon, our foreman said we're going to see how strong I am. And I carried 52 sacks of cement on my back-- 52 sacks from half past 2:00 till about quarter past 5:00. And that went on often, day after day. And I remember once, you do the same thing, and you carry the cement to a lorry. And the lorry is connected with a board, a wooden board, another wooden board sitting in the middle. So if you walk in the middle, you go up and down because it was too heavy, what I was carrying. One day, it fell off my back and split. And the Germans called it sabotage. The kapo used to hit me about it, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing I did to, to fight my hunger is after my-- I received my half a liter or 3/4 liter soup in my own barrack, I used to wander out and go to the other barracks, go inside the other barracks, and ask the kapos, can I sing some songs for the prisoners? And if the kapo said yes, do it, three, four songs I sang.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then if there was any soup left, they gave me some soup. I did it three or four barracks to fight my hunger. And if I had anything left for the next morning, I saved my piece of bread every morning for the afternoon and ate my soup or shared my soup with some of my Dutch friends that I had. Anything to keep going.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you have this experimentation done to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm co-- I'm come to that. This is still in Auschwitz. Before I leave Auschwitz, this happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the bad food and the condition, your body gets weaker and weaker. And I developed a big bump on my arm. And I wouldn't like to be in the hands of the doctor, so I massage it away. And it came up here again. I still got the mark here. See that? [POINTING] A big bump like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had bad feet. So for my feet, I was standing in a queue to the doctor to do something to it, but there was hardly any medicine. The things we had brought along, those thousands of people that brought medicine along from Holland, they were used up. All what they had is Vaseline. But I saw this bump, and the doctor put my number down-- my number on my arm-- to be operated. But I went back to my bunker, and I fell asleep. And later on, I heard my name c-- my number call, 98. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Because I-- whoever the doctor I had, oh, I should say I'm, I'm from London. I'm from London. I want to get out here, out here, thinking that somebody might say this Englishman ought not to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he called me out, and I had to be cut. I was cut. And the wound stays open, because it takes a long time before it's-- it heals. There was no linen bandage, only paper-- paper-- crepe paper around  it. But I had one here coming up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to one of my mates, look, I don't want to lay on the table again. He said, oh, I'll see to that. So he took a crochet needle and killed the root on the inside. That's what I had to do because I was afraid of the doctors. And I got a-- I got a operation in between my legs, probably from a kick from one of the kapos. But I couldn't help that. I couldn't walk, so  that had to be cut away.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was while you were still in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Auschwitz, yeah. Yeah, it's all in Auschwitz, what I'm saying, you know? Now then, so one morning, I'm queueing up for a bandage for this. And there's a man in a white coat looking at all the men, all stripped naked queueing up. And he looking at the body, and he looks at me, and he takes my number. I think nothing about it. I think nothing of it. And I goes to my bunk. And the next morning, I'm called out of my bunk by the same man. He was a doctor. He gives me a long coat, a pair of clogs, and I've got to follow him out of my barrack across into another barrack. And when I get into the barrack, I look, and I thought myself, this is a medical, medical department, clinic. So I see tubes and bottles and instruments laying about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I s-- this doctor says sit down, so I sit down. And there's another doctor there, and those two are messing about with instruments and talking to one another. Everyone was so-- I don't know what I'm doing here. So-- is this cold here? Is this cold? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I s-- I thought to myself, I'm going to ask. I says, what am I doing here? What am I here for? He says, we're going to use-- we're going to try out a new instrument. We want to have a look in your bladder. I said, I've got nothing the matter in my bladder. I said, look, I've got a hole in my arm here. He says, take it easy. [GERMAN] So I say, but I'm an Englishman. I ought to not be here. And my wife and child is here. Take it easy. Take it easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I take it easy. And I'm sitting there 20 minutes, maybe, when one doctor says to the other, he's not coming today. I'll-- we'll take him back. So I gets up, and they take me back to my barrack. And the next morning, the same thing happens. Out of my bunk, follow this man, and I get into the same barrack, and I'm told to sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm not sitting for a minute. The door opens, and in walks an SS soldier. And as I look, he's got the most beautiful uniform on-- must have been tailor made-- and decorations on his chest. And that's what I remember. For 50 years, I remember this uniform and the man in it-- not his face, but the decorations. And he walks past me without looking, and he goes and he stands in front of a window and looks out through the glass window. And he says in German to the doctors, [GERMAN], commence. Well, one doctor comes over to me. He straps my hands, with leather straps to the chair. I've got to widen my ne-- legs, strap into the legs so I couldn't move. And they put an instrument into my penis and pumped me full with-- it must have been water, a big bottle of water. And I let go, and the doctor says don't let go. [GERMAN], hold it. So I hold it. They take that thing out, and they put it back again. And they make it half light, the half light goes a bit dark. And they're messing about with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they're beginning to hurt me. I thought to myself, this is bad. God knows what they're doing. See, I was afraid, and the SS there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they're busy with me for about five or 10, 20 minutes-- I don't know-- when one doctor says loud enough that the SS can hear it-- I don't know if he did it on purpose or not. I wish I could find this doctor, but I don't know. He says, das est ein Englander. This is an Englishman. And he's not finished with the word Englishman when the SS turns away from the window, comes to me, and he says, you're British? And I hear a-- an American accent. I said, yes, sir. I says, I come from London. I'm a Londoner, born in London, and my wife and child is here. Can y-- I shouldn't be here. They ought to not be in here. Can you get us out? No, he says. You've got to put your complaint to the political department. I'm here to supervise the medical department. And away he goes to the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those doctors get busy with me. And all of a sudden, they hurt me so deep, and I thought to myself, I can't walk. I can't walk anymore after this. That is terrible. So I shout out in English, they're hurting me now! And without him moving away from the window, he says in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Let him go. He need not come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the doctor takes his things out of my body, unstraps me, and takes me back to my barrack. For the whole week, if I had to pee, blood came away from my body. And I didn't know, and I don't know yet what they'd done to me. This was just pure luck. If I wouldn't have shouted out, he wouldn't have known I was an Englishman. I wouldn't have been here.

speaker: interviewee
text: According to the books, they cut away the womens' vagina after doing something to it, send it off to Germany. In Germany, laboratorian looks what's happened. They cut away the testicles of the men, send it up to Germany, according to the book. I can let you read it. And a lot of men died, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 years later-- and this is only about two years ago-- I happened to look in a book, and it said the Auschwitz Nazi camp experimentations. And what do I find in there as I read it? Professor Clauberg and experimenting on the women, vagina this and the vagina that. And his assistant, Horst Schumann, the man who always operated in his Luftwaffe uniform, the man who operated in his Air Force uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a light goes up into me. This is the man who messed it up. He let the doctors mess about with me under his direction. So I knew that was Horst Schumann. He got, I think, 10 years. And then they had to stop because he became ill. They arrested him outside Germany somewhere, in the Indies.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I escaped from that, but I was a guinea pig. And all the men-- it must have been 50, 60 men in this ward-- they all had the same treatment. I didn't see them. So that was me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I left Auschwitz then with the 200 men to Monowitz. I lived 17 months in Monowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worked unloading the trains, unloading the trains of cement, sand, coal, rails, carrying and schlepping and kicking and beating and calling us names. And the trains arrived with all those trucks full of goods. Bricks, I carried a million bricks. And the engine was taken off. And we-- literally a kommando of 1,000 men-- 500 men on each side of the train with our shoulders-- the shoulder to the wagon and, and the-- and the kapo with a whistle. Every time he whistled, you had to push inch by inch the trains, all along until it stood where it could be unloaded. That's what the type of work it was. And another thing I remember, if you tried to escape or you did something wrong, and they hung you. It must have been July 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were marching back from work. And I don't know if you know, but you got the gate, Arbeit Macht Frei. And we-- we got to march through it. While we're marching, we're being counted. And that day, as we look, there's a large size window on the slant. And on that-- not window, a door, big sized door, and on that door is a body of a man. And he was shot in his chest, must have been shot in his chest. And in there, they planted a flower. And we, we had to look at that. And on another occasion, we march into the camp, and there were-- as I marched, I counted 12 gallows. And I thought, they're frightening us, German propaganda. No, they weren't frightening us. They meant it. And we stood there, and we're all being called-- 28,000 men in Auschwitz. And I was-- the short ones stood in the first row. And then behind us, you get the next, and the behind them so the SS could always see your heads and count you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was standing in the front row. And I was standing-- all my lot of the barrack were standing then very near to the gallows. And I said, look at the left. In the distance, I can see men coming along. And then as they come near, I can count 12 men-- big men, fit, clean, white, blue-striped uniform, hair clipped, shaven, clean, and marching. [INAUDIBLE]. And they pass [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a few yards from me, pass me, and that's where the gallows were, twelve gallows. Usually, there's three. Three gallows are still there. But they had to make another 9.

speaker: interviewee
text: It appears that two men had tried to run away and got in touch with civilian. They were caught. And for reprisal, they took nine other men. 12 men were hung. Well, they passed me by. And there were 12 little wooden boxes on the ground, and they had to stand on that. Their hands were tied behind. And then the camp kapo-- that's the chief manager of the whole camp-- he put the, the noose around the neck and kicked away the box. And the body fell, and they were choked. Number two saw what's happening to number one, then it was his turn. Kicked away the thing, then they dropped. Three and four, and I thought it was six or seven, the loop wasn't properly placed around his neck. And he was-- he was-- his body was swinging, and the tongue came out, and the face went blue and red. He was choking very slowly. They hadn't-- the loop wasn't-- the noose wasn't properly on his neck. So the kapo went over the body, pulled the body, and then it was tightened, and it became quiet. 12 men like that, and we had to watch it. And at the side of the gallows, two machine guns and SS. They could shoot you if anything-- if we would have done anything in reverse. We couldn't. 12 men, and then they were hung like puppies-- puppets. And then they were cut down, placed in a lorry, and wheeled away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they said into the barracks. And then we could eat our soup and forget about it. We couldn't do nothing. What could we do? You would have been shot down. And everybody wanted to live, so we j-- we just had to accept it. Only be careful that it's not you next time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw five being hung, three, two, shouting out the last moment, let us live. We will work. And the last one was, I think, in September '43, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '44, maybe. That was a young Frenchman. By the look of his face, I can still see him standing there. I don't think he even knew what was the matter with him. They hung him. After that, I didn't see any more hangings. Then it was January '45. The Russians were nearing Auschwitz.
